ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 11713004 is the closest parent for water as a substance; no specific mineral water product code used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 300 ml bottle of mineral water, as commonly sold at cafes and restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bottled Water, NFS — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for bottled/mineral water. Mineral water is essentially pure water with trace dissolved minerals; macronutrient values are confirmed zero. Sodium value reflects typical mineral water mineral content per USDA data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Water — University of Sydney GI Database convention for non-glycemic beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure water and mineral water contain no digestible carbohydrates and therefore have no measurable glycemic response. By convention, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) to satisfy validator requirements. gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance that water elicits no glycemic response.

hypertension.guidance: Mineral water contains approximately 2 mg sodium per 100 ml, which is nutritionally negligible and poses no concern for individuals managing hypertension; adequate hydration with water is actively encouraged in hypertension management guidelines.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Mineral water contains no carbohydrates or sugars and has a glycemic load of zero, making it the preferred hydration choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; it does not raise blood glucose and can be consumed freely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero sodium of clinical significance
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mineral water contains no fat, no cholesterol, and negligible sodium, and is therefore entirely safe and beneficial for individuals with cardiovascular disease; adequate water intake supports cardiovascular function and blood pressure regulation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mineral water is naturally sourced or processed water containing dissolved minerals such as calcium, magnesium, and trace amounts of potassium and sodium. It is bottled at source or after filtration and is sold as a still (non-carbonated) or sparkling beverage. This record refers to still mineral water as commonly sold in 300 ml bottles at cafes and food establishments in Singapore. Mineral water contains no macronutrients — zero calories, zero protein, zero fat, and zero carbohydrates — making it the ideal beverage for all dietary patterns including weight management, diabetes management, and cardiovascular health. The trace mineral content (calcium ~6 mg/100 ml, magnesium ~1 mg/100 ml, sodium ~2 mg/100 ml) contributes negligibly to daily mineral intake but does not raise any clinical concern. Mineral water has no glycemic impact whatsoever and is universally recommended as the primary hydration source across all clinical dietary guidelines. In Singapore, bottled mineral water is ubiquitous in cafes, restaurants, hawker centres, and convenience stores, and is commonly referred to as 矿泉水 (kuàng quán shuǐ) in Chinese, air mineral in Malay, கனிம நீர் in Tamil, and मिनरल वाटर in Hindi.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mineral water?
Is mineral water good for diabetes?
Can I drink mineral water if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of mineral water?
mineral water vs tap water nutrition

confidence_reason: Mineral water is a well-characterised food item with confirmed zero macronutrient values per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831). Nutrient values are universally consistent across all approved databases. No estimation or analogy was required for macronutrients.
notes: energy_kcal is naturally zero as mineral water contains no caloric macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero as water contains no protein. fat_g is naturally zero as water contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as water contains no carbohydrates. sugar_g is naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. All vitamin and most mineral fields are confirmed zero or trace per USDA FoodData Central. Sodium (2 mg/100 ml), calcium (6 mg/100 ml), potassium (1 mg/100 ml), and magnesium (1 mg/100 ml) reflect typical mineral water mineral content per USDA SR Legacy data. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. The cuisine field in the input was listed as 'Italian' due to the restaurant context (The Light Cafe — High Street Plaza), but mineral water is a generic global beverage and has been reclassified as 'Generic' with region_of_origin 'Global'. The 300 ml serving size is preserved from the input description. Multilingual names are provided for Singaporean context given the field survey data source, covering Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required by the multilingual matrix for Singaporean food records.

improvement_actions_taken: Reclassified cuisine from 'Italian' (restaurant context artifact) to 'Generic' with region_of_origin 'Global'.
Set glycemic_index to 1 (sentinel positive value) per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule.
Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes field for all four core macros.
Populated full multilingual names for Singaporean context (Chinese Simplified/Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list — all entries present verbatim.
Selected USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved nutrient source.
Assigned defensible FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT ontology codes with honest match types.
